query: AO principles of fixation with adequate bone buttressing

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: External fixation is a relatively safe minimally invasive procedure and can provide significant benefit to the patient when used in the correct setting. As such, there are limited contraindications for its use in orthopedics. Relative contraindications include an obese patient where safely placing pins would be difficult. A non-compliant patient is a relative contraindication because he or she may not follow up for the removal of the device. Also, peri-prosthetic fractures can limit the bone stock available to place the pins. General contraindications include patient refusal, unable to withstand the procedure physiologically.

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Technique or Treatment -- Pelvis
retrieved_docs.content: External fixation in the pelvis is common for both provisional and definitive fixation. The two main pin locations are the iliac wing and the anterior inferior iliac spine. For iliac wing pins, a small incision is made to bone approximately 2 cm posterior to the anterior superior iliac spine. The target insertion site is the gluteal ridge because this is the strongest and widest portion of the iliac wing. Upon breaching the cortex, the pin can be inserted manually between the inner and outer cortex. A second pin can be placed just posterior to the first pin to increase stability. Penetration through the outer table is minimized by use of a blunt tip but is tolerable because of the inherent protection of the gluteal musculature. This process can be done quickly in experienced hands and with limited fluoroscopy. Supracetabular pins are sometimes better tolerated by the patient and have better control of the pelvis than iliac wing pins. [12] However, safe placement of supracetabular pins requires fluoroscopic guidance.  Supracetabular pins get placed in the dense bone from the anterior inferior iliac spine to the posterior superior iliac spine. The fluoroscopic marker of this corridor is called the "teardrop." The teardrop is about 2 cm above the acetabular dome. The obturator outlet view on intraoperative fluoroscopy is useful for identifying the teardrop. The obturator inlet view shows the pin traversing in between the inner and outer table and can show the depth of the screw. The iliac oblique view shows the depth and trajectory of the pin. The pin on this view should be directed 1 to 2 cm above the sciatic notch to protect the sciatic nerve and gluteal vessels. [7] Posterior obturator stabilization is achievable with pelvic C-clamps contacting the posterior ilium. The C-clamp can be applied quickly and without any fluoroscopic guidance. Care must be taken with concomitant sacral comminution as this can lead to over-compression and sacral nerve injury.

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications and contraindications of external fixation. Describe the equipment, biomechanics, and technique in regards to external fixation. Review appropriate evaluation of the potential complications and clinical significance of external fixation. Explain interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance external fixation treatment and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Acetabular Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Reduction and Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: Except for both column fractures, standard fracture reduction sequence is first to reduce and stabilize displaced column fractures and then reduce any wall fracture After definitive fixation of reduced fragments, entire construct stabilized with buttress plates For both column fractures, first reduce and stabilize one of the columns to the axial skeleton (iliac wing), then the other column, and later, if present, the wall component. The entire construct then gets stabilized with buttress plates Columns may be stabilized in young, healthy bone using screws alone; osteopenic bone and all wall fractures require buttress plating

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians have been using external fixation to treat fractures for more than 2000 years after being first described by Hippocrates as a way to immobilize the fracture while preserving soft tissue integrity. The fixator design and biomechanics have changed dramatically over the years, but the principles remain the same. The primary goal of external fixation is to maintain the length, alignment, and rotation of the fracture. External fixation can serve as provisional fixation or definitive fixation purposes. Both methods can be performed in conjunction with partial internal fixation if necessary. It is important for orthopedic surgeons at a trauma center to be familiar with the techniques and principles of external fixation for various fractures of the upper extremity, lower extremity, and pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: Pathologic Fractures -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical intervention for a pathologic fracture should only be considered following an exhaustive oncological work-up and biopsy of the lesion. General principles of internal fixation are applicable but may differ when a fracture of pathologic nature is involved. For instance, implant selection should generally adhere to the following guidelines: Load-sharing as opposed to load-bearing when possible Durable implants with the goal of lasting the length of patient survival with disease progression Immediate stability allowing for immediate weight-bearing Length of implant should bypass the lesion by two cortical diameters Cement augmentation when necessary

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: External fixation has been used for the treatment of fractures for 2000 years. The application and devises have changed, but the biomechanics remain the same. This activity outlines the relevant anatomy, techniques, and fixation goals and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients who undergo external fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians use external fixation in orthopedic trauma, pediatric orthopedics, and plastic surgery for an array of different pathology. Below are a few of the indications for external fixation devices: Unstable pelvic ring injuries Comminuted periarticular fractures such as pilon, distal femur, tibial plateau, elbow, and distal radius fractures Fractures with large amounts of soft tissue swelling Fractures in a patient that is hemodynamically unstable or cannot undergo an open procedure Comminuted long bone fractures Fractures with significant bone loss Open fractures with soft tissue loss Limb deformity and limb lengthening Osteomyelitis with bone loss Immobilization of joint after soft tissue flap Arthrodesis Nonunion Malunion Infection Traction to aid in intraoperative fracture reduction

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction -- External Fixator Types
retrieved_docs.content: There are many different ways to change and enhance the external fixation construct. To complicate things further, there are also hybrid frames which are a combination of any of the previous constructs described. The surgeon must create a level of stability that is appropriate for optimal healing. It is essential also to have a good understanding of basic fracture principals because stiffer is not always better when it comes to external fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: When a limited number of bony segments are present, the order in which they are fixated is not necessarily critical; the general principle is to secure mobile segments to stable ones. [32] [33] [34] When comminuted panfacial fractures occur, it may be helpful to proceed from lateral to medial, using the zygomatic arches to establish the width of the face. Then, the naso-orbito-ethmoid region and maxillary alveolar ridge can be used to set midfacial projection. Last, the midface's anterior buttresses and the mandible's rami will determine the facial height.

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture healing physiology largely depends on the mode of fixation and level of stability. With absolute fracture stability such as compression plating, the bone will undergo primary intramembranous bone healing. On the other hand, relative fracture stability, such as external fixation, results in secondary enchondral bone healing. There are also several ways to alter the external fixation construct to make the fracture more or less stable.

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are two main methods of external fixation of the pelvis. The iliac crest site can be easily palpated and found quickly. The clinician makes a small incision just proximal to the gluteal ridge to preserve the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. Pin placement here is safe as long as the inner and outer tables of the pelvis are not penetrated. Anterior superior iliac spine pins require direct fluoroscopic visualization as the starting point and trajectory are more difficult to find. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Principle of Tendon Transfers -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Graft (Donor) Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: Recent innovations have addressed the challenge of combining strength with a lower profile. A notable example is the side-to-side coaptation technique, which has demonstrated superior strength without increased bulk, making it a promising option for early mobilization scenarios. This development represents a significant step in balancing the need for strong fixation with the desire for less invasive and bulky repairs. [5] Parallel to these advancements in tendon-to-tendon fixation, tendon-to-bone fixation has gained popularity, particularly in lower extremity procedures. This shift is largely due to improvements in implant technology. Techniques using bone anchors or interference screws are now preferred for their reduced risk of wound complications and less extensive surgical dissection.

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: External fixation plays a vital role in fracture care today. Not only can it temporarily stabilize fractures, but it can provide definitive fixation as well. The use of external fixation in a damage control setting can prevent the so-called “second hit” phenomenon because of the quick application, decreased blood loss, and minimally invasive application. Furthermore, by giving time for the soft tissues swelling to subside in severe injuries, the risk of injection and wound complication proportionally decreases.

By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment for both fracture or dislocation of the hip and femur and for fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity other than the hip or femur. This procedure is closely related to the Arabic‑language concept تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of fracture), which is considered the inverse of the English term, indicating that they represent reciprocal perspectives on the same surgical approach. Moreover, Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific form of Internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction, highlighting its applicability to clavicular injuries when reduction is not performed. Both تثبيت داخلي للكسر and إعاضة رأس الفخذ (hip head replacement) are listed as members of the SMQ category هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (osteoporosis/low bone mass), underscoring the clinical relevance of these surgical interventions in patients with compromised bone quality. Together, these relationships illustrate how internal fixation procedures, including those without fracture reduction, fit within broader treatment categories for lower‑extremity injuries and are particularly pertinent to the management of osteoporotic bone conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 227
retrieved_docs.content: fixation of fracture). Both internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction and تثبيت داخلي للكسر are members of the osteoporosis/low bone mass SMQ, linking these fixation techniques to patients with osteoporotic bone. Additional concepts associated with the osteoporosis SMQ include “إعانة رأس الفخذ” (hip‑head assistance or prosthesis) and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures), indicating that hip‑head prosthetic support and the presence of multiple fractures are recognized clinical manifestations or treatment considerations in the context of low bone density. Collectively, these entities and relationships illustrate a comprehensive framework for the surgical and therapeutic management of musculoskeletal conditions, particularly those involving the clavicle, shoulder‑arm region, and femur, and emphasize the pivotal role of bone quality in guiding operative and non‑operative strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur. Within this framework a full spectrum of femoral procedures is organized as child entities, including growth‑modulating techniques such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus).” Direct operative interventions on the femur comprise “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” “Osteoplasty of femur,”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 167
retrieved_docs.content: Additional concepts that belong to this SMQ include تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (hip‑head prosthesis), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures), all of which are clinical entities commonly managed in the setting of osteoporotic bone. Together, these relationships illustrate how various closed fractures—whether of the femur, skull, humerus, scapula, or ulna—are interconnected through shared morphology, common etiologic links to osteoporosis, and overlapping classifications within intracranial and skeletal injury hierarchies, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding injury patterns and guiding patient‑centered management.

retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

This integrated structure aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical strategies, recognizing the influence of bone health on fracture risk, and documenting procedures within standardized orthopedic classification systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: femur**. The procedure **internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction** (Arabic: تثبيت داخلي للكسر), a subclass of internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction, is applied to both “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur” and “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” when reduction is not performed. Both this fixation technique and **hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are members of the osteoporosis/low bone density grouping (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)), highlighting the importance of bone quality in fixation and replacement surgeries. Collectively, these entities illustrate how the THIGH AND LEG surgical domain integrates fracture stabilization, deformity correction, limb‑length alteration, prosthetic revision, and reconstructive strategies centered on the femur and adjacent lower‑extremity bones, providing a unified framework for the management of complex thigh and leg pathologies.
